Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after work, but with limited resources and needing to share devices at home, it never really stuck. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me feel like I was at least absorbing something. Some weeks I'd manage 19 hours, other weeks barely anything at all.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I'd cram before deadlines rather than keeping a steady schedule. When things got overwhelming with work or family stuff, studying was the first thing to slip. I wanted to be more consistent, but life just kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or wanting to catch up on discussion posts that got me to log in. I actually enjoyed the discussions and interacting with classmates, so sometimes that was motivation enough. Other times I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, even if I didn't end up doing much productive work.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became almost automatic, like checking social media. I'd log in pretty frequently, maybe out of anxiety about missing something important. But it wasn't always productive—sometimes I'd just scroll through without really engaging deeply. The habit formed more from stress than from genuine routine.
Q5: The video lectures were really valuable for me since I learn best by listening. I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, to really absorb the concepts. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives helped clarify things. Those two resources felt like they actually contributed to my understanding.
Q6: I tended to ignore or delay the reading materials and some of the written resources. With my auditory learning style, dense text just didn't stick as well. Plus, with limited internet access sometimes, downloading and reading PDFs felt like a chore. I knew they were important, but I kept putting them off.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see an immediate connection to the assignments or when it helped clear up confusion I had. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, even better. Activities that felt disconnected from what I actually needed to do for grades often got pushed aside.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were easier to skip when I was stressed or short on time. Required ones had that pressure of consequences, so I'd prioritize those even when exhausted. I actually enjoyed some optional discussions, but when things got tight, they were the first to go.
Q9: Yeah, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture audio and listen on my phone during commutes or while working. I also borrowed textbooks from friends since I couldn't afford my own. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were times when I'd be reviewing notes I'd taken by hand or re-listening to downloaded lectures for hours, but the LMS wouldn't show any of that. I'd also study with classmates in person, quizzing each other verbally. My LMS activity would look dead during those periods even though I was genuinely working.
Q11: Definitely. I'd often access a video once, download the audio or take detailed notes, and then never touch that LMS page again. With my internet being unreliable sometimes, it made more sense to grab what I needed and work offline. The system wouldn't know I spent hours with that material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My stress levels were through the roof, and I just couldn't focus. I was surprised by how quickly I fell behind—one week of struggling turned into a snowball effect. The CBT sessions helped a bit with the anxiety, but by then I'd already missed too much.
Q13: Absolutely. Working to make ends meet while studying with low resources meant I was constantly juggling. Fatigue was a big factor—I'd log into the LMS exhausted and just not absorb anything. Confusion about course material piled up because I couldn't always attend live sessions to ask questions.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or other responsibilities. I'd technically be logged in and active, but I wasn't really there mentally. It's frustrating because the time spent didn't translate to actual learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was anxiety—the bigger the task seemed, the more I'd avoid it. My assignment completion rate shows that pretty clearly, I think.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have bursts of activity right before due dates, cramming everything in at once. Without that pressure, I'd drift and lose focus. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only way I could force myself to produce anything.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it actually motivated me to engage more. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I felt disconnected from the course. My assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted, so positive feedback reminded me I could do this if I just showed up.
Q18: The course structure was a bit rigid for someone with my schedule. Fixed deadlines didn't account for weeks when life got chaotic. I appreciated having recorded lectures available, but the attendance expectations were tough to meet. More flexibility would have helped me stay on track.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier—I genuinely liked those interactions. But the heavy reliance on reading materials made things harder for me as an auditory learner. Also, when resources required constant internet access, that was a barrier given my situation at home.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. I was highly engaged in terms of logging in, watching videos, and participating in discussions, but my actual submission rate was low. It's like I was present but couldn't convert that presence into completed work due to stress and other barriers.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone downloads everything early and studies offline, their LMS activity would look minimal but they could still learn effectively. I tried to do that, but my exam performance shows I didn't quite succeed. Still, the concept makes sense to me.
Q22: Course logs can show frequency and duration of access pretty accurately. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone's actually focused or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline studying and the emotional state behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's showing up mentally, asking questions, and connecting with the content. For me, it also means overcoming barriers to participate, even when circumstances make it hard. It's more than just clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the hours spent listening to downloaded lectures or the stress that made focusing nearly impossible. It also wouldn't show that I actually cared about this course—I just couldn't always demonstrate that in measurable ways.
